seo.title: RabbitMQ Queue Management — Purge, Delete, Create & Inspect | Qarote
seo.description: Manage RabbitMQ queues from a clean UI: purge, delete, create queues, inspect bindings, view exchange topology, and publish test messages — no CLI access required.

hero.title: Manage your RabbitMQ queues without touching the CLI.
hero.subtitle: Purge queues, delete exchanges, create bindings, and publish test messages directly from the Qarote dashboard. The operations you reach for during incidents and deployments — available to your whole team, without SSH access to the broker.

operations.heading: Every queue operation in one place

items.title: Purge queues
items.description: Drain a queue's messages in one click. Useful during incident recovery when you need to clear a stuck queue without waiting for consumers to process a backlog — or when you've deployed a bad message format and need a clean slate.

items.title: Delete queues
items.description: Remove queues that are no longer needed. Delete with confirmation — Qarote shows you the current message count before you commit, so you know whether you're deleting an empty queue or discarding live messages.

items.title: Create queues
items.description: Create new queues with full property control: durable, exclusive, auto-delete, TTL, max length, dead-letter exchange. The same options available via the management plugin — in a cleaner interface.

items.title: Inspect bindings
items.description: View all bindings for any queue: which exchanges route to it, with which routing keys. Binding inspection is essential for debugging routing issues — understanding why messages are or aren't arriving in a queue.

items.title: Publish test messages
items.description: Publish a message to any exchange with a custom routing key and payload directly from the dashboard. Test your routing configuration, verify consumer behavior, or trigger a workflow without writing code.

items.title: Topology view
items.description: Visual overview of exchanges, queues, and bindings across your broker. Understand your messaging architecture at a glance — especially useful when inheriting a system or onboarding new team members.

steps.title: Open any queue
steps.description: Navigate to any queue in your Qarote dashboard. All queues on all connected servers are listed automatically — no configuration, no allowlist.

steps.title: Select an operation
steps.description: Purge, delete, create, or publish from the queue detail view. Destructive operations show a confirmation dialog with current queue state — so you always know what you're about to do.

steps.title: Changes apply immediately
steps.description: Operations are executed against the RabbitMQ management API in real time. The queue list refreshes automatically after each operation — you see the result immediately.

whyCLI.heading: CLI access creates operational bottlenecks
whyCLI.p1: RabbitMQ's `rabbitmqctl` gives you full queue management capability — on the broker server, with root or rabbitmq user access. In practice, this means queue management is gated behind SSH access to production servers. When an on-call engineer needs to purge a stuck queue at 2am, the first step is finding the right SSH key and jumping through a bastion host.
whyCLI.p2: The management plugin's UI is an improvement — but it's accessible only on port 15672, which most organizations restrict. It also provides no audit trail: there's no record of who purged what queue, when, and why.
whyCLI.p3: Qarote gives your whole team a single place for queue management, controlled by Qarote's user permission model. Queue operations are available to team members with the appropriate Qarote role — no broker access required, no SSH keys to distribute.

items.question: Who can perform queue management operations?
items.answer: Queue management permissions are controlled by Qarote's role system. Admins can perform all operations. Read-only users can inspect queues and bindings but cannot purge, delete, or create. You control who has write access.

items.question: Is there an audit log for queue operations?
items.answer: Queue management operations are logged in Qarote's activity log — including which user performed the operation, which queue was affected, and when. The audit log is available on Developer and Enterprise plans.

items.question: Can I purge a queue without losing the messages permanently?
items.answer: Purge is destructive — messages are removed without being delivered to consumers. If you need to move messages rather than discard them, use the management plugin's move-messages feature or a consumer that republishes to another queue. Qarote's purge is a one-way operation.

items.question: Does queue management work with quorum queues?
items.answer: Yes. Qarote supports classic queues, quorum queues, and stream queues. Queue creation supports quorum queue properties (replication factor, delivery limit). Some operations behave differently for quorum queues — for example, the management API enforces quorum queue constraints on creation.

items.question: Can I manage exchanges and bindings directly?
items.answer: Yes — you can view all exchanges, inspect their bindings, and create or delete bindings from the topology view. Direct exchange management (create/delete) is available on the exchanges tab within any connected server.
